quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Product rule"?

options.text: Applying the power rule term-by-term: d/dx[3x⁴ - 2x² + 5x - 1] = 12x³ - 4x + 5
d/dx[f(x)g(x)] = f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x); 'first times derivative of second plus second times derivative of first'
Use a constraint equation to express the objective function in terms of one variable before finding critical points
0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞-∞, 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰; L'Hôpital's directly handles the first two

quiz_questions.explanation: "Product rule" is defined as: d/dx[f(x)g(x)] = f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x); 'first times derivative of second plus second times derivative of first'. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Quotient rule"?

options.text: A function can have more than one point satisfying MVT; each is where the tangent is parallel to the secant
d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f'(x); constants factor out of the derivative
d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x)] / [g(x)]²; 'low d-high minus high d-low over low squared'
d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n; includes x⁻¹ = 1/x, x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3), etc.

quiz_questions.explanation: "Quotient rule" is defined as: d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x)] / [g(x)]²; 'low d-high minus high d-low over low squared'. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Chain rule"?

options.text: Uses the midpoint of each subinterval: M_n = Σf((xᵢ + xᵢ₊₁)/2)Δx; generally more accurate than left/right
f''(x) > 0; the graph curves upward like a cup; the slope is increasing
f' gives the topology (hills and valleys); f'' gives the shape (cupping up or down); together they determine the complete curve shape
d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) · g'(x); differentiate the outer function evaluated at the inner, then multiply by the derivative of the inner

quiz_questions.explanation: "Chain rule" is defined as: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) · g'(x); differentiate the outer function evaluated at the inner, then multiply by the derivative of the inner. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Nested chain rule"?

options.text: f'(x) represents the instantaneous rate of change of whatever f(x) models: population, cost, temperature, concentration
For triply composed functions: d/dx[f(g(h(x)))] = f'(g(h(x))) · g'(h(x)) · h'(x)
First derivative test: always works but requires sign analysis on an interval; second derivative test: simpler but can be inconclusive
∫csc²x dx = -cot x + C; ∫sec x tan x dx = sec x + C; ∫csc x cot x dx = -csc x + C

quiz_questions.explanation: "Nested chain rule" is defined as: For triply composed functions: d/dx[f(g(h(x)))] = f'(g(h(x))) · g'(h(x)) · h'(x). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "When to use which rule"?

options.text: f'(x) > 0: f is increasing (quantity growing); f'(x) < 0: f is decreasing (quantity declining)
Product rule: two functions multiplied; Quotient rule: division; Chain rule: composition (function inside another function)
Left-hand limit: lim(x→c⁻) f(x); right-hand limit: lim(x→c⁺) f(x); the two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal
Change of base: logₐ x = ln x / ln a, so d/dx[logₐ x] = 1/(x ln a)

quiz_questions.explanation: "When to use which rule" is defined as: Product rule: two functions multiplied; Quotient rule: division; Chain rule: composition (function inside another function). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "d/dx[f(x)g(x)] = f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x); 'first times derivative of second plus second times derivative of first'"?

options.text: d/dx[cos x] = -sin x
Epsilon (ε)
Product rule
Second derivative test

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Product rule".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x)] / [g(x)]²; 'low d-high minus high d-low over low squared'"?

options.text: Bounding function changes
Integration by parts indicators
Angular rate problems

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Quotient rule".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) · g'(x); differentiate the outer function evaluated at the inner, then multiply by the derivative of the inner"?

options.text: Inflection point at P = K/2
Chain rule
Interval additivity
Cumulative distribution function (CDF)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Chain rule".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "For triply composed functions: d/dx[f(g(h(x)))] = f'(g(h(x))) · g'(h(x)) · h'(x)"?

options.text: Rectilinear motion
Nested chain rule

Separation process

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Nested chain rule".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Product rule: two functions multiplied; Quotient rule: division; Chain rule: composition (function inside another function)"?

d/dx[ln x] = 1/x

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules is accurate?

options.text: Linear approximation uses the tangent line to estimate function values near a known point: f(x) ≈ f(a) + f'(a)(x-a).
This technique is essential for functions like xˣ, (sin x)^x, or x^(1/x) where both base and exponent are variable.
Drug dosing models use DEs to track drug concentration in the bloodstream.
Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules are the essential rules for differentiating composite expressions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules?

options.text: The power rule: d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n.
These derivatives are essential for modeling growth, decay, and natural phenomena.
Phase line analysis reveals stability: P = 0 is unstable (arrows point away), P = K is stable (arrows point toward).
The product rule: d/dx[f·g] = f'g + fg'.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules?

options.text: The quotient rule: d/dx[f/g] = (f'g - fg')/g².
The sign of the derivative indicates whether the quantity is increasing or decreasing, and the second derivative reveals acceleration or deceleration of change.
Continuous compound interest follows A = Pe^(rt).
U-Substitution (Part 2): Advanced Techniques handles more complex substitution scenarios.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules as covered in this lesson.

options.text: The power rule: d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n.
The chain rule: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x))·g'(x).
Integration by parts may need to be applied more than once.
Basic Derivative Rules provide shortcuts for finding derivatives without using the limit definition every time.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules?

options.text: When k > 0: exponential growth (population, investment); when k < 0: exponential decay (radioactive decay, drug clearance).
Energy conservation connects work to kinetic and potential energy changes.
These three rules, combined with basic derivatives, enable differentiation of virtually any function.
Continuity and Types of Discontinuity classifies discontinuities into three types.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules?

options.text: Numerical integration in computer science: trapezoidal and Simpson's rules are programmed into calculators and software for approximating integrals when antiderivatives don't exist in closed form
Financial mathematics: differentiating revenue functions R(x) to find marginal revenue and determine the optimal quantity to maximize profit
Finding the rate of change of kinetic energy KE = ½mv²: d(KE)/dt = mv·(dv/dt) = mva, using the chain rule with velocity as an intermediate variable
Calculating cable length for suspension bridges: the cable hangs in a catenary curve y = a·cosh(x/a), and arc length integration determines the required cable length

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules?

options.text: AC electrical circuits: voltage V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt) has derivative V'(t) = V₀ω cos(ωt), the rate of voltage change used in capacitor/inductor analysis
Climate data: identifying periods of warming (increasing temperature trend) vs. cooling using derivative analysis of temperature time series
Compound interest: if A = P·e^(rt), then dA/dt = rP·e^(rt) = rA, showing the growth rate is proportional to the current amount
Aquarium design: the total force on viewing windows increases dramatically with depth, requiring thicker glass for deeper tanks

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Optics: the rate of change of image position with respect to object position in a lens system requires the chain rule applied to the thin lens equation
Speedometer readings as instantaneous velocity: the derivative of position with respect to time at each moment, computed electronically in modern vehicles
Quality control: setting an acceptable error margin (ε) for a product property and determining the input precision (δ) needed in the manufacturing process
AP Calculus free-response questions consistently test the ability to set up integrals from verbal descriptions and geometric reasoning

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules?

options.text: Financial markets: the 'Greeks' (delta, gamma, theta) in options pricing are derivatives that measure how option value changes with respect to stock price, rate of change, and time
Finding the rate of change of kinetic energy KE = ½mv²: d(KE)/dt = mv·(dv/dt) = mva, using the chain rule with velocity as an intermediate variable
Medical research: sketching dose-response curves to identify therapeutic windows (effective dose ranges) for drug development
Long-term drug concentration models: horizontal asymptotes represent the steady-state drug level that the body approaches with regular dosing

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules in practice?

options.text: Profit maximization: after finding the critical point of P(x), checking P''(x) < 0 confirms it's a maximum profit level, not a minimum
Medical research: sketching dose-response curves to identify therapeutic windows (effective dose ranges) for drug development
Long-term drug concentration models: horizontal asymptotes represent the steady-state drug level that the body approaches with regular dosing
Compound interest: if A = P·e^(rt), then dA/dt = rP·e^(rt) = rA, showing the growth rate is proportional to the current amount

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Product, Quotient, and Chain Rules.
